title: Lesson 352
transcript: All right, in this video, we have a few more quick definitional things that we have to go through, edge computing, embedded devices, and HPCs, high performance computing. So let's start with edge computing. And this is the one we're actually gonna spend just a couple of minutes on here. So the idea of edge computing is to get computational power as close to the user as possible. And the whole reason for this, and this is funny because we're recording some CISSP and CCSP videos here today, and I just talked about this earlier today, but I'll explain it again. We talked about it for our CCSP class. So what is edge computing? Edge computing is this general term where you want to try and get computational power, both computational power and storage, as close to the user as possible. And that's what edge computing means, getting computational power and storage as close to the user as possible. A subset of edge computing is what's known as a CDN, a content distribution network. And let me explain that because it's probably the easiest to understand and then I can generalize to edge computing. So who remembers the video Gangnam Style? Anyone? Yeah? Alright, that's pretty awesome music video, right? Oh, even John, alright, excellent. So, here's why I always remember this, okay? So, I think Gangnam Style was the very first YouTube video to hit a billion views. When Gangnam Style was in its heyday, there was literally millions of people watching that video simultaneously. Imagine the bandwidth requirements for that. Like, yes, Google has vast data centers, but even for Google, trying to stream, you know, trying to have like say 10 million simultaneous streams of like a 1080p video out of one data center in Utah would strain even Google's resources. So how did they do this? How were they able to simultaneously stream this same video to millions of users simultaneously? Well, they did it because they have a CDN, a content distribution network. Google and other big cloud providers and other companies like Akamai, for instance, they pay money to put their servers in telecom companies' data centers. So I've actually seen them. You'll be walking through an AT&T data center and you'll be like, hey, there's a Facebook server, there's a Netflix server, there's a Google server. And you might wonder, like, why are... Why is there a Google server? Why is there a Netflix server in an AT&T data center or a Verizon data center or a Telus data center? And the answer is that these are CDN servers, content distribution network servers. They are basically just storage servers. And the reason that they're in these telecom data centers is because they're really close to the endpoint user. So for instance, say you live in New York City, all the big cloud providers and other companies like Akamai, they will have content distribution network servers, basically caching servers, in the telecom office that's probably just a few miles from your house. And that way, when you pressed play on the Gangnam style video, it would have played near instantaneously because it wasn't streaming from halfway across the planet. It was streaming to you from like 10 miles down the road from your house. So the huge advantage of a CDN is that it massively reduces the latency. When a customer wants to watch a video, they can do so very quickly because the file is probably only a few miles from their house. So it really improves performance. it also helps to enormously save bandwidth. Like, say in New York City where there's 10,000 people all simultaneously watching the Gangnam Style video, all of those 10,000 simultaneous streams don't have to go across the internet backbone. Instead, those 10,000 streams are just happening from a small number of servers just a few miles from each of those people's houses. And all that traffic is not going across the internet backbone. You know, the big cloud service provider just basically has to copy the file once to their CDN server, and then it can be streamed a whole bunch of times, viewed a whole bunch of times by users, local users nearby. So that's the idea of a content distribution network. Massively improves performance and helps save kind of core internet backbone bandwidth. And as I mentioned, a CDN is a type of edge computing. Edge computing is more of a general term where edge computing is not only about bringing data storage, but also computational power as close to the user as possible. Next little, very brief thing we have to cover here is this idea of an embedded device. An embedded device is simply a device that's a combination of hardware and software for a very specific function. Think pacemaker. A pacemaker is a perfect example of an embedded device. It's a highly specialized piece of hardware and software for a very specific function keeping your heart rate regulated. And this kind of highlights why the security of embedded devices is very important, because they're often used in pretty mission-critical situations, like say pacemakers. So that's embedded devices. And then HPC, high performance computing. This is simply supercomputers. If you see this term at all on the exam, HPC, high performance computing, it just means supercomputers. Thank you.
